main_summary.summary: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, interviewed by John Dittmer in Baltimore on March 18, 2013, traces her path from a leftist West Indian household in Providence, Rhode Island into SNCC field organizing in Georgia and Alabama. Her father, an optometrist born in St. Vincent, served as Rhode Island state chairman of the Progressive Party and faced deportation threats after Henry A. Wallace's 1948 campaign; HUAC pressure drove a family friend to suicide. After enrolling at the University of Michigan in 1961, she joined SDS, attended the April 1962 SDS-SNCC meeting in Chapel Hill, and worked with Bernard Lafayette Jr. and the Detroit Friends of SNCC during Wayne State summer classes with C.L.R. James's Facing Reality circle (George Rawick, Seymour Faber). In summer 1963 she defied her parents and joined Charles Sherrod's Southwest Georgia Project in Albany before going to Greenwood, Mississippi to work with Sam Block and Willie Peacock on voter registration. She attended the 1963 March on Washington, the 1964 MFDP challenge in Atlantic City, and joined the Selma project in early 1965, working on poverty-fund challenges, union drives at the Pepsi plant, and voter canvassing through the Selma-to-Montgomery period. She co-edited Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC (Illinois, 2010).
key_themes: SNCC voter registration in Georgia and Mississippi
Progressive Party family heritage and Cold War repression
Preventive nonviolence and armed community defense
main_summary.historical_significance: Noonan is one of a small number of Northern Black women who joined SNCC field projects as undergraduates between 1962 and 1965, and her account provides first-person testimony on three distinct SNCC theaters: Sherrod's Albany/Southwest Georgia project, the Greenwood Mississippi voter-registration drive under Sam Block and Willie Peacock, and the Selma ward-by-ward organizing alongside the SCLC voting-rights campaign. Her testimony documents the 1948 Progressive Party deportation threat against her West Indian-born father, the Detroit Friends of SNCC circle around Bernard Lafayette Jr. and C.L.R. James's Facing Reality group, the Fay Bellamy account of the Forman-Carmichael pre-Bloody-Sunday meeting at the Selma dentist's house, and the Lowndes County Freedom Organization founding discussion where she argued for a black star rather than the panther symbol. As co-editor of Hands on the Freedom Plow, she also speaks to the documentary recovery of SNCC women's accounts.

chapters.title: You Look Like a Nice Man
chapters.summary: Interviewed by John Dittmer in Baltimore in March 2013, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan opens with her family in Providence: a Saint Vincent-born optometrist father active in Pan-West-Indian unity, a Michigan-born mother descended from a Colored Conventions minister who settled in Buxton, Ontario, both Progressive Party members, and her own first political act at age three, walking up to presidential candidate Henry A. Wallace and landing on the front page of the Providence Journal.

chapters.title: I'm Black and I'm Proud
chapters.summary: She recounts the price of her parents' activism: an FBI or immigration agent threatening to deport her naturalized father, a jewelry-factory owner called before HUAC who committed suicide, the family buying a former funeral parlor in an unwelcoming white neighborhood where stones came through the window and she was beaten going to school, and her mother teaching her at six to answer slurs with the line that she was Black and proud of it.

chapters.title: The Only Black Student
chapters.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan describes being the lone Black child at Lexington Avenue Elementary, then the only Black student in the entire Lincoln School for Girls, a Quaker prep school her mother secured a scholarship to, the neighbor who came for an eye exam as a gesture of welcome and used the same prescription forty years later, and being barred from the Glee Club lest she socialize with boys at the joint prep-school concerts.

chapters.title: A Member of the Rosa Parks Generation
chapters.topic: A Northern Child and the Movement
chapters.summary: Her parents kept Emmett Till's murder from her until college, but she identified instead as a child of the Rosa Parks generation, fixing on Daisy Bates, the Little Rock Nine, and Autherine Lucy integrating schools, and crediting the Harriet Tubman biography her mother gave her, the first book she owned, alongside spirituals and an early lesson to distrust the history taught in school.

chapters.title: Take It With a Grain of Salt
chapters.summary: She remembers a mother who sang opera and spirituals, played organ, and taught her to question the official history and even the pulpit, citing Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John to note no women wrote the Gospels, and describes the small Winter Street AME Zion congregation where she accompanied her sightless father to trustee meetings and learned how an organization handles its finances and rules of order.

chapters.title: Two Years Apart for a Daughter
chapters.topic: Getting to the University of Michigan
chapters.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan explains how her mother, a Michigan graduate at seventeen, moved to Detroit and lived apart from her father for two years so her daughter would qualify for in-state tuition at the University of Michigan, the school the family could afford, even as the daughter had wanted Fisk after seeing a white paper on the Nashville movement.

chapters.title: Your People Are in Motion All Over the South
chapters.topic: SDS and a Trip to Chapel Hill
chapters.summary: She recalls joining the Methodist Students Association and meeting Curtis Hayes from McComb, Mississippi at a Friday brown-bag event, joining SDS as the only group talking about racism and peace, and Tom Hayden, back from a Freedom Ride in Albany, pressing her to leave peace work and inviting her carload to an SDS-SNCC meeting in Chapel Hill in April 1962 that she calls an extraordinary experience.

chapters.title: Representing the Race
chapters.summary: She describes her parents' adamant refusal to let her go South at seventeen, rooted in her mother's own law-school experience at Michigan, where she believed Black students were flunked out once the school realized its mistake, and the general expectation among the small group of Black families that their children would become professionals, keep their noses clean, and honor the sacrifices made for their education.

chapters.title: Friends of SNCC in Detroit
chapters.topic: Summer Support Work at Wayne State
chapters.summary: Instead of going South in 1962, she enrolled at Wayne State and spent afternoons with the Friends of SNCC group built around Bernard Lafayette, fundraiser sent by SNCC, alongside Freedom Rider Elizabeth Hirschfeld and Detroit activists like John Watson and Luke Tripp, who were reading Robert F. Williams and Negroes With Guns and would later take over the Wayne State South End paper, all doing support work for the students in the South.

chapters.title: She Left Michigan in Labor
chapters.summary: She recounts the summer's culminating event, bringing Diane Nash to Detroit while Nash was under indictment in Mississippi and nine months pregnant, her tagline that any Black child in Mississippi was in jail already, and how Bernard Lafayette put Nash forward rather than himself, then setting up a Friends of SNCC group in Ann Arbor with the Freedom Singers until her parents, seeing she barely knew where her classes were, cut off her money and she took on her own education.

chapters.title: I Thought the Bad Things Were Over
chapters.summary: Slipping away South in 1963 to Charles Sherrod's Albany project, she describes orientation in Sumter County and the murder there of a young woman raped by thirteen white men, one a bread-route driver who parked his truck outside her house, a horror that shattered her belief the worst was past and made her fear the same could happen to her, amid still-large mass meetings led by Sherrod with Reverend Samuel Wells and C.B. King.

chapters.title: I Would Hate to Talk to Your Brother
chapters.summary: She recalls taking sanctuary in a church guarded against carloads of armed white men by JoAnn Christian's father and cousin with rifles across their laps, her estranged mother working through the Deltas up to Burke Marshall and Robert Kennedy with a warning she would hate to have to talk to his brother, and the preventive nonviolence she first saw in Albany, where Sherrod tipped off local gangs to position themselves between the white crowd and the nonviolent marchers.

chapters.title: A Bumblebee Car Into Greenwood
chapters.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan tells how Sherrod had tricked them into staying by warning Mississippi was too dangerous after Medgar Evers, so when he left town she and Jean Wheeler bused to Greenwood, where Sam Block and Willie Peacock met them in a yellow-and-black car and drove eighty miles an hour, and she spent the summer canvassing for a voting day that drew two or three hundred people though almost no one was allowed to register.

chapters.title: The Culture of the Movement Was Black
chapters.summary: Comparing Sherrod's Albany project and Greenwood, she notes both had only a handful of white volunteers, who in Mississippi never left the office for safety, and argues that what drew her was a Black-led movement, young Black people in charge of their own destiny as she had first seen in Chapel Hill, with Sherrod in command and Prathia Hall taking over when he left, not a fifty-fifty integration.

chapters.title: Encouraged to Be as Much as I Could Be
chapters.summary: Asked about the treatment of women in the movement, Prescod Norman Noonan says she never felt mistreated, only encouraged to do as much as the men, felt herself a novice in what was a war zone to her, and observed that in the minutes of meetings, where no one was put down by gender, you often could not tell which speaker was a woman and which a man.

chapters.title: A Soldier in the Civil Rights Army
chapters.summary: She recalls the 1963 March on Washington feeling too civilized after Mississippi, identifying with the hard-pressed people from Danville and Cambridge, Maryland, sitting up front as honored guests, the censoring of John Lewis's speech, the few women on the program, and her dismay that Roy Wilkins made a disparaging remark about W.E.B. Du Bois, who had died that very day.

chapters.title: She Faked a Heart Attack
chapters.summary: She tells of going back North to reconcile with her parents after the march and her mother, knowing Wayne State had not yet started, faking a heart attack to keep her home, later admitting she would have done anything to keep her out of Mississippi, and returns to John Lewis's censored speech, thought too radical for its line about marching through the South like Sherman and its first-draft charge that Kennedy's civil rights bill was too little and too late.

chapters.title: I Know What Democracy Is
chapters.summary: She explains why she skipped Freedom Summer in 1964, opposing the summer project as much of the Mississippi staff did because Black organizers in the Black community was what worked and they were not recruiting Black students, then recounts Atlantic City that August, proud Michigan's was the first Democratic Party to back the MFDP, where a Council of Churches man defended the two-seat compromise red-faced before Fannie Lou Hamer rose to say the people should choose their own representatives, not an appointed Ed King and Aaron Henry.

chapters.title: We Blew It Wide Open
chapters.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan reflects that in roughly two years of activism with almost no money the movement had made the vote a national issue, that through a small historical opening they forced change wide open, and invokes Mahalia Jackson singing Joshua Fit the Battle of Jericho as her image of leaning on the walls of segregation until they came tumbling down.

chapters.title: Twelve Dollars for a Six-Day Week
chapters.summary: Joining the Alabama SNCC project in early 1965, she describes weekend work while studying Black college students' vocational aspirations with Pat Gurin, Doris Derby, and John Bracey at Tuskegee, then being assigned a ward of Selma to do whatever the people wanted, from a stoplight to getting Black women hired at the Dan River Mills against a day-work wage of twelve dollars a week, and helping Pepsi-plant men unionize with an AFL-CIO organizer who came at night.

chapters.title: We Were Community Organizers
chapters.summary: As King and the SCLC came to Selma to dramatize the right to vote, Prescod Norman Noonan voices her suspicion that liberals after the MFDP challenge wanted SCLC to blunt a populist SNCC, citing a New York meeting with Allard Lowenstein, and describes the uneasy coexistence, eating together at Walker's Cafe, a SNCC and an SCLC person assigned to each ward, with SCLC promoting demonstrations while SNCC did local groundwork.

chapters.title: The U-Turn March
chapters.summary: She recounts that SNCC officially opposed supporting the march to Montgomery, though individuals like John Lewis and Bob Mants joined, and that after Bloody Sunday everyone came to Selma for the U-turn march, when SCLC's leadership stopped at the bridge, prayed, and led the marchers back to Brown Chapel under a deal, and relays the account in Hands on the Freedom Plow of Forman and Stokely pleading with King to break the federal injunction.

chapters.title: The Streets Were Full
chapters.summary: Rather than march, Prescod Norman Noonan went to the parallel SNCC demonstrations in Montgomery, where University of Michigan students came down through a Tuskegee tie, left before things got rough as a community organizer rather than a demonstrator, and returned the day the march culminated to feed people at a church by the Capitol amid streets so full of participating community people the cars could not pass.

chapters.part: Lowndes County and a Black Star
chapters.title: People Were Going in Droves
chapters.summary: After the march she kept canvassing and tried to organize Selma registrants by ward, work that turned administrative once federal registrars arrived and people went to register in droves without difficulty, even as the movement's energy and Stokely Carmichael shifted out to Lowndes County, Alabama and the independent Black political party that became the Lowndes County Freedom Organization.

chapters.part: Lowndes County and a Black Star
chapters.title: I Wanted a Black Star
chapters.topic: A Debate Over the Panther Symbol
chapters.summary: She describes visiting Lowndes for an early mass meeting where people already had a practiced arrangement for getting home safely, and a Boy Scout camp discussion of the independent party's symbol, where she favored a black star over the black panther, linking it through Marcus Garvey's Black Star Line and Nkrumah's Ghanaian airline to the African struggle, while admitting this set her apart from her colleagues' thinking on Black institutions.

chapters.part: Lowndes County and a Black Star
chapters.title: Across the Street From the Jail
chapters.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan recalls that challenging the poverty funds brought a cordial Mayor Smitherman to the table, that the SNCC office sat directly across from the police station so they could see people jailed through the window, that it was state troopers, not Selma police, who beat marchers on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, and that public safety director Wilson Baker, who had set up that office placement, once sent cars to bring them home safely on Halloween.

chapters.title: I'll Wait for You
chapters.summary: She tells of meeting her first husband on the Selma project, marrying in 1967 as he faced orders to Vietnam and won conscientious-objector standing as an officer, her pledge to follow him to Canada, Africa, or wait through jail, and his mother's blunt warning against crossing an international boundary or marrying a felon, then how a visit to Sherrod's New Communities farm near Albany, the house riddled with bullet holes, sent the couple toward medical school and a history PhD instead of risking the baby's life.

chapters.title: Something Old, Not Something New
chapters.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan admits she often stayed outside SNCC meetings to talk with people from other projects, found the internal tensions easily resolvable, and saw the changes not as something new but as a return to a SNCC she had always known as a Black organization, with the Mississippi Summer Project as the aberration and the later turn a homecoming.

chapters.title: Organize, Organize, Organize
chapters.summary: She recounts her integrationist parents hosting a party after Stokely Carmichael's Black Power speech, her mother telling him she was glad they finally got some sense and to organize, and explains she was never surprised or turned off by the slogan because she had always understood the work as building vehicles of Black power and radicalizing the whole political structure, while regretting a missed chance to unite the Southern movements.

chapters.title: A New Set of Dangers
chapters.summary: Asked about the last forty-five years, she describes studying and teaching history, a PhD left unfinished amid family emergencies, anti-hunger and sickle-cell tutorial projects, post-SNCC retrospective conferences and freeing Curtis Hayes from jail in Liberia, and raising three sons in inner-city Detroit amid police harassment and gun violence that made at least four women in her circle lose sons, the sons now a lawyer and two physicians.

chapters.title: Hands on the Freedom Plow
chapters.topic: A Book of SNCC Women's Stories
chapters.summary: Prescod Norman Noonan closes describing the collection she helped compile, Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC, published by the University of Illinois Press in 2010, gathering the stories of fifty-two women active in SNCC-connected civil rights work, most of them college students at the time but also middle-aged activists, white and Black women, and two Hispanic women.

loc_video.caption: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan oral history interview conducted by John Dittmer in Cockeysville, Maryland, 2013 March 18
